title: determinism.mjs serialize() omits fire field — napalm determinism not covered
evidence: determinism.mjs serialize() at lines 76–89 constructs a canonical object with phase/turn/wind/winner/tanks/projectiles/lastExplosion/explosions/terrain but does NOT include the `fire: FireCell[]` field added to GameState (GameState.ts:101–108). A napalm-fire determinism regression — where two runs produce differing fire-cell arrays — would produce identical serialize() output and pass the check silently. The scripted ACTIONS in determinism.mjs fire a missile (non-napalm), so the `fire` array is always [] in the tested scenario; the gap is structural, not a current failure.
impact: A future engine change that introduces a napalm-fire non-determinism would not be caught by the determinism harness, because the fire field is excluded from the canonical comparison.
recommendation: Add `fire: state.fire` to the canonical object in serialize() in determinism.mjs. Consider adding a second check variant that fires a napalm shot and asserts the fire field is non-empty and identical across both runs.
acceptance_criteria: a napalm-shot determinism check is present that asserts fire.length > 0 in at least one run

title: determinism.mjs tank serialization omits post-Sprint-4/5/6 fields — can hide multi-field regressions
evidence: The tank serializer at lines 67–75 only captures {id,x,y,angle,power,health,alive}. TankState now has: powerCap (line 170), shieldHp (line 188), fuel (line 175), credits (line 205), inventory (line 179), roundWins (line 213), kills (line 219), totalDamage (line 229), buried (line 237), buriedTurns (line 239). A divergence in any of these fields between two deterministic runs would not be caught by the byte-comparison. Compare with batteries.mjs:208 and scoreboard.mjs:107 which include these fields in THEIR serializers — demonstrating the determinism.mjs serializer is stale relative to them.
impact: A non-determinism bug in credits, inventory, powerCap, shieldHp, kills, totalDamage, roundWins, buried, or buriedTurns would pass the determinism check invisibly. These fields are all engine-computed and thus part of the determinism claim.
recommendation: Expand the tank snapshot in determinism.mjs serialize() to include all TankState fields that the engine computes deterministically: powerCap, shieldHp, credits, inventory (serialize as JSON), fuel, kills, totalDamage, roundWins, buried, buriedTurns.
acceptance_criteria: tank snapshot in determinism.mjs serialize() includes at minimum: powerCap, shieldHp, credits, kills, totalDamage, roundWins, buried

title: determinism.mjs serialize() omits round/totalRounds/lastRoundWinnerId — multi-round determinism blind spot
evidence: serialize() at line 76–89 includes `turn` but not `round` (GameState.ts line 21), `totalRounds` (line 29), or `lastRoundWinnerId` (line 39). The determinism check runs a single-round game (default options), so these fields are trivially equal across both runs (round=1, totalRounds=1, lastRoundWinnerId=null). netrounds.mjs and rounds.mjs both assert on these fields for multi-round scenarios, but the primary determinism harness would not catch a regression that causes round-counter drift in a single-round game.
impact: If the round counter or lastRoundWinnerId diverges between two engines replaying the same log, the determinism check would not detect it. Low current risk (single-round scenario), but grows if the default GameEngine ever initialises with rounds>1.
recommendation: Add round, totalRounds, and lastRoundWinnerId to the canonical object in serialize().
acceptance_criteria: serialize() includes round, totalRounds, and lastRoundWinnerId

title: index.test.ts only tests bare-number RPC result — array form documented in production code is untested
evidence: index.test.ts test case (a) at line 23 injects `{data: 7, error: null}` — a bare scalar. The production rpcResultToResponse at lines 37–40 explicitly handles BOTH a bare number AND an array (`Array.isArray(data) ? data[0] : data`), with the comment 'PostgREST may deliver it as a bare number or a single-element array.' The array variant (`{data: [7], error: null}`) is not tested. If PostgREST's behaviour is exclusively array-form (or switches to array-form in a future supabase-js version), the bare-number path in the test is testing a scenario that real PostgREST does not produce.
impact: The Array.isArray branch is untested. If PostgREST returns `[7]` and the bare-number handling were ever accidentally broken, all three tests would still pass.
recommendation: Add a test case: `rpcResultToResponse({ data: [7], error: null })` → 200 `{ seq: 7, ok: true }` to exercise the array-extraction branch.
acceptance_criteria: A test exists for data=[7] (array form) yielding seq:7

title: validate.test.ts human() fixture omits lastSeen — StoredPlayer rows in production always carry lastSeen
evidence: The test helper human() at lines 21–23 returns `{id, name, color, ready: true}`, omitting `lastSeen`. In production every join_room (join_room/index.ts:104), create_room (index.ts:122), ready_up (ready_up/index.ts:49), heartbeat (heartbeat/index.ts:50), update_player (update_player/index.ts:96), and restart_game (restart_game/index.ts:133) all set `lastSeen: nowMs`. StoredPlayer declares lastSeen as optional (mod.ts line 187), so the TypeScript type is consistent with omission — no compiler error. authorizeAction() does not read lastSeen (it reads only .id and .ai), so no functional gap exists today. The risk is future code added to authorizeAction that reads lastSeen could silently fail to be tested.
impact: Low today because authorizeAction ignores lastSeen. Risk grows if validation logic ever gates on lastSeen (e.g. staleness check in the referee).
recommendation: Add `lastSeen: Date.now()` to the human() helper in validate.test.ts to match the shape every real create/join path produces, keeping the fixture honest.

title: list_rooms.test.ts player() fixture omits lastSeen — real roster players always have it, reap() uses it
evidence: The list_rooms.test.ts player() helper at line 13–15 returns `{id, name, color, ready: true}` with no `lastSeen`. The test passes this fixture directly to mapListedRoom(), which does not call reap(). However, in the real list_rooms/index.ts (line 27), the roster is passed through `reap(players, nowMs)` BEFORE being handed to mapListedRoom(). reap() at mod.ts lines 309–311 filters `p => (p.lastSeen ?? 0) >= nowMs - STALE_MS`. Without lastSeen, `p.lastSeen ?? 0` evaluates to 0, which is always < nowMs - 30000 — so a real player row with no lastSeen would be reaped immediately. The test exercises mapListedRoom in isolation and thereby sidesteps the reap path entirely; a bug in the reap→mapListedRoom pipeline would be invisible.
impact: The tests validate mapListedRoom in isolation. A regression where reap() incorrectly removes live players before mapListedRoom sees them (e.g. the stale window calculation changes) would not be caught. Also, if mapListedRoom were ever refactored to read lastSeen, tests with no lastSeen would give false confidence.
recommendation: The player() helper should include `lastSeen: Date.now()` to match the actual StoredPlayer shape that passes through reap(). Ideally, add an integration-style test that calls reap() then mapListedRoom() with both stale and fresh players to verify the full pipeline.
acceptance_criteria: at least one test exercises the reap→mapListedRoom path with mixed stale/fresh players
